If we had tested it, we would have caught the defect.
We would have shipped on schedule.
If we had hired her earlier, we would not be firefighting now.
We could have salvaged the launch.
If we had run a pilot, we would have caught the problem.
If the forecast had been realistic, we would have ordered less stock.
We could have salvaged it if we had acted sooner.
Izabel, thanks for running this. The Halo launch missed target by thirty percent. Where do you land on the root cause?
Speed. We rushed. In hindsight, if we had run a regional pilot, we would have caught the pricing problem before the full rollout.
And the firmware defect? That was the setback customers actually noticed.
Exactly. If we had tested with real customers, we would have found it in a week. We underestimated how visible it would be.
Should we write off the whole line, then?
No. There is real momentum in the technology. If we had not built the brand so carefully, there would be nothing to salvage -- but there is.
So what is the turning point here -- what changes for the next launch?
Process, not luck. If we had hired an operations lead a year earlier, we would not be firefighting now. So we fix the process, and the next launch will not backfire the same way.
If we had run a pilot, we would have caught the pricing problem.
If we had tested with customers, we would have found the defect.
The root cause was speed: we misjudged the market.
There is still momentum, so we can salvage the line.

Izabel, it is Roland from the board, with my honest read on the Halo launch before the review. In hindsight, the shortfall was avoidable. If we had run a small pilot in one region first, we would have caught the pricing problem before the full rollout. We underestimated the competition, and we overlooked how fast they would cut their prices. The root cause was speed: we misjudged the market because we rushed the decision. Here is the part that still hurts, and it is a mixed one. If we had hired an operations lead a year earlier, we would not be firefighting today. I am not writing the product off. There is real momentum in the technology, and I believe we can salvage the line. But this review is the turning point. If we do not learn from it, the next launch will backfire the same way. Call me before we meet.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Sofia, your new operations lead, with the root-cause note you asked for on Halo. Let me be precise about what went wrong. The headline is a thirty percent shortfall against target. If the forecast had been realistic, we would have ordered fewer components and avoided the excess stock. We also misjudged the lead times. If the supplier had flagged the delay earlier, we would have shipped on schedule. Here is the setback that hurt the most, a firmware defect. If we had tested with real customers, we would have found it in a week. In hindsight, none of this was bad luck; it was process. The good news is momentum. If we had not built the brand so carefully, there would be nothing to salvage now, but there is. My recommendation is simple: fix the process, and the next launch will not backfire. I will walk you through the fixes on Thursday.
